rawText:
Surveys appear in news headlines almost every day — "78% of workers prefer remote work," "most parents support later school hours" — yet the numbers they produce deserve far more skepticism than they usually receive.

The first question to ask about any survey is who was asked, because the composition of the sample determines whether the results can be generalized to a broader population.

An online poll shared on a technology forum will attract respondents who are already comfortable with technology, which means the results may say more about that community than about the public as a whole.

Professional polling organizations address this problem by using random or stratified sampling methods designed to mirror the demographics of the population they claim to represent.

Even with careful sampling, the way a question is worded can dramatically shape the answers it produces.

Asking "Do you support funding for public parks?" will yield a very different result from asking "Should taxpayers pay more to maintain parks?" — both touch the same topic, but the framing activates different associations in the respondent's mind.

Researchers call this "question-order effect" and "framing bias," and reputable surveys go through multiple rounds of testing to minimize these distortions.

The margin of error is another detail that headlines almost always omit but that readers should always look for.

A survey reporting that 52% of people favor a policy, with a margin of error of plus or minus four percentage points, is actually saying that the true figure could be anywhere between 48% and 56% — which means the result is essentially a coin flip.

Response rates matter as well: if only fifteen percent of the people contacted actually completed the survey, the opinions of the silent eighty-five percent remain unknown, and there is no guarantee that they share the same views.

Finally, consider who funded the survey and what they might have hoped to find, because organizations sometimes design studies to produce results that support a predetermined conclusion.

None of this means that surveys are useless — when conducted rigorously, they remain one of the best tools for understanding public opinion at scale.

The point is not to dismiss every number but to develop the habit of asking a few simple questions before accepting it as fact.

A well-informed reader treats survey data the way a careful driver treats a yellow traffic light — not as a signal to stop, but as a reminder to slow down and look more carefully before proceeding.

overview.title: How to Read a Survey Without Being Fooled

sentences.text: We see survey numbers in the news every day, but we should not believe them right away.
sentences.translation: 우리는 매일 뉴스에서 설문조사 숫자를 보지만, 바로 믿으면 안 돼요.

sentences.text: It matters a lot who was asked the questions, because different people give different answers.
sentences.translation: 누구에게 질문했는지가 아주 중요해요, 왜냐하면 다른 사람들은 다른 답을 하거든요.

sentences.text: The way you ask a question can change the answer you get.
sentences.translation: 질문하는 방식이 받는 답을 바꿀 수 있어요.

sentences.text: When a survey says "52% agree," the real number might be higher or lower.
sentences.translation: 설문조사가 "52%가 동의한다"라고 말해도, 실제 숫자는 더 높거나 낮을 수 있어요.

sentences.text: We should also think about who paid for the survey, because they might want a certain answer.
sentences.translation: 설문조사 비용을 누가 냈는지도 생각해야 해요, 왜냐하면 특정 답을 원할 수도 있거든요.

sentences.text: Surveys are still useful, but a smart reader always asks questions before trusting the numbers.
sentences.translation: 설문조사는 여전히 유용하지만, 똑똑한 독자는 숫자를 믿기 전에 항상 질문을 해 봐요.

sentences.text: The article argues that survey results deserve more skepticism than most people give them.
sentences.translation: 이 글은 설문조사 결과가 대부분의 사람들이 주는 것보다 더 많은 의심을 받을 만하다고 주장해요.

sentences.text: Who was included in the sample determines whether the findings apply to the general public or just a specific group.
sentences.translation: 표본에 누가 포함되었는지가 그 결과가 일반 대중에게 적용되는지 아니면 특정 집단에만 해당되는지를 결정해요.

sentences.text: Question wording and framing can significantly alter survey results, even when the underlying topic is the same.
sentences.translation: 질문의 문구와 프레이밍은 근본 주제가 같더라도 설문조사 결과를 크게 바꿀 수 있어요.

sentences.text: The margin of error means that a reported percentage is actually a range, not a precise figure.
sentences.translation: 오차 범위는 보도된 비율이 실제로는 정확한 수치가 아니라 범위라는 것을 의미해요.

sentences.text: Low response rates and potential funder bias are additional reasons to examine survey claims carefully.
sentences.translation: 낮은 응답률과 잠재적 출자자 편향은 설문조사 주장을 신중하게 검토해야 하는 추가적인 이유예요.

sentences.text: The essay concludes that surveys remain valuable when read critically, like a driver approaching a yellow light with caution.
sentences.translation: 이 에세이는 설문조사가 비판적으로 읽힐 때 여전히 가치 있다고 결론짓는데, 마치 운전자가 노란 신호등을 조심스럽게 대하는 것과 같아요.

sentences.text: This expository essay systematically deconstructs the common practice of accepting survey statistics at face value, using a sequential argument structure.
sentences.translation: 이 설명적 에세이는 설문조사 통계를 액면 그대로 받아들이는 일반적 관행을 순차적 논증 구조를 사용해 체계적으로 해체해요.

sentences.text: The writer moves through sampling bias, question framing, margin of error, response rates, and funder motivation — each serving as a distinct lens for evaluating survey credibility.
sentences.translation: 글쓴이는 표본 편향, 질문 프레이밍, 오차 범위, 응답률, 출자자 동기를 차례로 거치며, 각각이 설문조사 신뢰성을 평가하는 독립적인 렌즈 역할을 해요.

sentences.text: Hedging language — "may say more," "can dramatically shape," "essentially a coin flip" — allows the writer to question without overstating, modeling the very caution the essay advocates.
sentences.translation: 완화 표현 — "더 많이 말해줄 수도 있다", "극적으로 형성할 수 있다", "본질적으로 동전 던지기" — 은 글쓴이가 과장 없이 의문을 제기할 수 있게 하며, 에세이가 옹호하는 바로 그 신중함을 스스로 보여줘요.

sentences.text: Concrete numerical examples — 52%, plus or minus four points, fifteen percent response rate — ground abstract statistical concepts in tangible figures.
sentences.translation: 구체적 수치 예시 — 52%, 플러스마이너스 4포인트, 15% 응답률 — 가 추상적 통계 개념을 실감 나는 수치로 기초를 잡아줘요.

sentences.text: The essay uses a not-to-dismiss-but-to-question rhetorical stance, balancing accessibility with intellectual rigor throughout.
sentences.translation: 이 에세이는 무시하려는 것이 아니라 의문을 제기하려는 수사적 입장을 사용하며, 전반에 걸쳐 접근성과 지적 엄밀함의 균형을 유지해요.

sentences.text: The concluding analogy of a yellow traffic light elegantly encapsulates the essay's central message: proceed, but with informed caution.
sentences.translation: 노란 신호등이라는 결론적 비유가 에세이의 핵심 메시지를 우아하게 요약해요: 진행하되, 정보에 기반한 신중함으로.

sentences.original: Surveys appear in news headlines almost every day — "78% of workers prefer remote work," "most parents support later school hours" — yet the numbers they produce deserve far more skepticism than they usually receive.
sentences.translation: 설문조사는 거의 매일 뉴스 헤드라인에 등장한다 — "근로자의 78%가 재택근무를 선호한다", "대부분의 부모가 등교 시간 연기를 지지한다" — 그러나 이 조사들이 만들어 내는 숫자는 보통 받는 것보다 훨씬 더 많은 회의적 시각을 받을 만하다.

grammarElements.text: in news headlines almost every day
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (장소/빈도)

grammarElements.text: "78% of workers prefer remote work," "most parents support later school hours"
grammarElements.label: 대시 삽입구 (구체적 예시)

grammarElements.text: yet the numbers they produce deserve far more skepticism than they usually receive
grammarElements.label: yet 양보절 (비교급)

vocabulary.example: Surveys can reveal public opinion on important issues.
The scandal dominated the headlines for weeks.
Healthy skepticism is essential for evaluating claims.

expressions.meaning: 훨씬 더 많은 의심을 받을 만하다
expressions.usage: 어떤 정보가 비판적으로 검토되어야 함을 강조할 때 쓰는 격식적 표현
빈도를 나타내는 일상적 부사구

patterns.pattern: [Subject] appear in [context] — [example A], [example B] — yet [they] deserve far more [critical response] than they usually receive
patterns.explanation: 구체적 예시를 대시 삽입구로 제시한 후, yet으로 전환하여 비판적 시각의 필요성을 도입하는 에세이 개시 패턴.
examples: Statistics appear in advertisements almost everywhere — "9 out of 10 doctors recommend" — yet the claims deserve far more scrutiny than they usually receive.
Expert opinions appear in talk shows every week — "the economy will recover," "prices will fall" — yet the predictions deserve far more questioning than they usually receive.

drillQuestions.question: The numbers surveys produce deserve far more _____ than they usually receive.
"Yet" in this sentence signals a _____ between appearance and reality.
"78% of workers prefer remote work" is an example of a survey _____.
"Far more skepticism" uses "far" to _____ the comparative.

sentences.notes: 글의 도입부로, 대시 삽입구 안에 구체적인 설문조사 헤드라인 예시를 넣어 독자의 친숙함을 활용한 뒤, yet으로 전환하여 비판적 읽기의 필요성을 즉시 제시한다. "far more skepticism"의 비교급 강조가 에세이 전체의 논조를 설정한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Surveys appear in news headlines almost every day
koreanAlignment.ko: 설문조사는 거의 매일 뉴스 헤드라인에 등장한다

koreanAlignment.en: "78% of workers prefer remote work," "most parents support later school hours"
koreanAlignment.ko: "근로자의 78%가 재택근무를 선호한다", "대부분의 부모가 등교 시간 연기를 지지한다"

koreanAlignment.en: yet the numbers they produce
koreanAlignment.ko: 그러나 이 조사들이 만들어 내는 숫자는

koreanAlignment.en: deserve far more skepticism
koreanAlignment.ko: 훨씬 더 많은 회의적 시각을 받을 만하다

koreanAlignment.en: than they usually receive

pronunciationNotes: skepticism: /ˈskeptɪsɪzəm/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "스켑티시즘"에 가깝다. sk를 한 번에 발음.
headlines: /ˈhedlaɪnz/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "헤드라인즈"에 가깝다. head의 ea는 /e/로 발음.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 예시를 통한 문제 제기 (problem introduction via examples)

sentences.original: The first question to ask about any survey is who was asked, because the composition of the sample determines whether the results can be generalized to a broader population.
sentences.translation: 어떤 설문조사에 대해서든 가장 먼저 물어야 할 질문은 누구에게 질문했는지인데, 표본의 구성이 그 결과를 더 넓은 모집단에 일반화할 수 있는지를 결정하기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: The first question to ask about any survey
grammarElements.label: 주어 (to 부정사 수식 명사구)

grammarElements.text: because the composition of the sample determines whether the results can be generalized to a broader population
grammarElements.label: because 이유절 (whether 명사절 포함)

vocabulary.example: The composition of the jury raised concerns about fairness.
The sample included five hundred randomly selected adults.
Results from a small group cannot be generalized to everyone.

expressions.usage: 우선순위를 명시하며 체계적 분석을 시작할 때 쓰는 표현

expressions.meaning: 더 넓은 모집단에 일반화할 수 있는
expressions.usage: 표본에서 전체로 추론하는 통계적 개념을 표현하는 학술적 표현

patterns.pattern: The first question to ask about [X] is [wh-question], because [factor] determines whether [results] can be [applied] to [larger group]
patterns.explanation: 비판적 분석의 첫 번째 단계를 제시하고, because절로 그 이유를 일반화 가능성과 연결하는 패턴.
examples: The first question to ask about any experiment is who participated, because the selection of subjects determines whether the findings can be applied to the general public.
The first question to ask about any study is how the data was collected, because the methodology determines whether the conclusions can be trusted by other researchers.

drillQuestions.question: The first question about any survey is who was _____.
The composition of the _____ determines whether results can be generalized.
"Generalized to a broader population" means applied to a _____ group.
"The first question to ask" sets up a _____ approach to reading.

sentences.notes: 체계적 비판의 첫 번째 기준(표본)을 도입하는 핵심 문장. "the first question"으로 우선순위를 설정하고, because절 안에 whether 명사절을 포함시켜 일반화 가능성이라는 통계적 개념을 자연스럽게 설명한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The first question to ask about any survey
koreanAlignment.ko: 어떤 설문조사에 대해서든 가장 먼저 물어야 할 질문은

koreanAlignment.en: is who was asked
because the composition of the sample
determines whether the results can be generalized
koreanAlignment.ko: 그 결과를 일반화할 수 있는지를 결정하기 때문이다

koreanAlignment.en: to a broader population

pronunciationNotes: composition: /ˌkɑːmpəˈzɪʃən/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "캄퍼지션"에 가깝다.
generalized: /ˈdʒenərəlaɪzd/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "제너럴라이즈드"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: An online poll shared on a technology forum will attract respondents who are already comfortable with technology, which means the results may say more about that community than about the public as a whole.
sentences.translation: 기술 포럼에서 공유된 온라인 여론조사는 이미 기술에 익숙한 응답자들을 끌어들일 것이며, 이는 그 결과가 전체 대중보다 해당 커뮤니티에 대해 더 많은 것을 말해줄 수 있다는 것을 의미한다.

grammarElements.text: An online poll shared on a technology forum
grammarElements.label: 주어 (과거분사 수식 명사구)

grammarElements.text: respondents who are already comfortable with technology
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (관계절 포함)

grammarElements.text: which means the results may say more about that community than about the public as a whole
grammarElements.label: 계속적 관계절 (비교급)

vocabulary.example: An online poll is not the same as a scientific survey.
Only thirty percent of respondents completed the questionnaire.
Not everyone is comfortable with new technology.

expressions.phrase: the results may say more about
expressions.meaning: 결과가 ~에 대해 더 많이 말해줄 수도 있다
expressions.usage: 데이터가 의도한 것과 다른 대상을 반영할 수 있다는 뉘앙스를 전달하는 완화 표현
일반 대중 전체를 가리키는 격식적 표현

patterns.pattern: [A biased sample source] will attract [specific group], which means the results may say more about [that group] than about [the intended population]
patterns.explanation: 편향된 표본 수집 방식의 구체적 예시를 제시하고, 계속적 관계절로 그 함의를 풀어내는 패턴. may가 신중한 추론을 나타낸다.
examples: A questionnaire distributed at a gym will attract respondents who already exercise regularly, which means the results may say more about fitness enthusiasts than about average adults.
A survey posted on a parenting blog will attract readers who are already engaged parents, which means the results may say more about that audience than about parents in general.

drillQuestions.question: An online poll on a technology forum will attract respondents who are already _____ with technology.
The results may say more about that community than about the _____ as a whole.
"Which means" introduces a _____ of the previous statement.
"Respondents" are people who _____ a survey.

sentences.notes: 표본 편향의 구체적 사례를 제시하는 문장. 과거분사(shared on a technology forum)가 주어를 수식하고, 계속적 관계절(which means ~)로 표본 편향이 결과에 미치는 영향을 설명한다. "may say more"의 완화 표현이 신중한 추론을 보여준다.

koreanAlignment.en: An online poll shared on a technology forum
will attract respondents who are already comfortable with technology
koreanAlignment.ko: 이미 기술에 익숙한 응답자들을 끌어들일 것이며

koreanAlignment.en: which means the results may say more
koreanAlignment.ko: 이는 그 결과가 더 많은 것을 말해줄 수 있다는 것을 의미한다

koreanAlignment.en: about that community
than about the public as a whole

pronunciationNotes: respondents: /rɪˈspɑːndənts/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "리스판던츠"에 가깝다.
technology: /tekˈnɑːlədʒi/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "테크날러지"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Professional polling organizations address this problem by using random or stratified sampling methods designed to mirror the demographics of the population they claim to represent.
sentences.translation: 전문 여론조사 기관들은 자신들이 대표한다고 주장하는 모집단의 인구통계를 반영하도록 설계된 무작위 또는 층화 표집 방법을 사용해 이 문제를 해결한다.

grammarElements.text: by using random or stratified sampling methods
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (수단)

grammarElements.text: designed to mirror the demographics of the population they claim to represent
grammarElements.label: 과거분사구 + 관계절 수식

vocabulary.example: Polling organizations conduct surveys before elections.
Stratified sampling ensures all subgroups are represented.
The demographics of the city have changed over the past decade.

expressions.usage: 문제에 대한 공식적 대응을 설명할 때 쓰는 격식적 표현
방법론이 특정 목적에 맞게 만들어졌음을 나타내는 학술적 표현

patterns.pattern: [Professional organizations] address this problem by using [method A or method B] designed to [objective]
patterns.explanation: 문제 제기 후 전문가 집단의 해결책을 "by using" 전치사구와 과거분사 수식으로 설명하는 패턴.
examples: Research institutions address this problem by using double-blind or placebo-controlled designs intended to eliminate experimenter bias.
Quality control teams address this problem by using automated or manual inspection processes designed to catch defects before shipping.

drillQuestions.question: Professional polling organizations address the problem by using random or _____ sampling methods.
These methods are designed to _____ the demographics of the population.
"Demographics" refers to the _____ characteristics of a population.
"Address this problem" means to _____ this problem.

sentences.notes: 편향 문제에 대한 전문적 해결책을 제시하는 문장. "random or stratified sampling methods"라는 기술적 용어를 도입하며, 과거분사(designed to mirror)와 관계절(they claim to represent)이 중첩된 고급 구조를 보여준다.

koreanAlignment.en: Professional polling organizations
address this problem
by using random or stratified sampling methods
koreanAlignment.ko: 무작위 또는 층화 표집 방법을 사용해

koreanAlignment.en: designed to mirror the demographics
of the population they claim to represent

pronunciationNotes: stratified: /ˈstrætɪfaɪd/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "스트래티파이드"에 가깝다. str을 한 번에 발음.
demographics: /ˌdeməˈɡræfɪks/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "데머그래픽스"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Even with careful sampling, the way a question is worded can dramatically shape the answers it produces.
sentences.translation: 신중한 표집을 하더라도, 질문이 어떻게 표현되는지가 그것이 만들어 내는 답을 극적으로 형성할 수 있다.

grammarElements.text: the way a question is worded
grammarElements.label: 주어 (the way + 수동태 관계절)

vocabulary.example: Careful sampling is essential for reliable survey results.
The question was worded in a way that suggested the answer.
A small change in wording can dramatically alter the response.

expressions.usage: "even with"로 양보를 나타내며, 앞서 논의한 해결책만으로는 충분하지 않음을 시사

expressions.phrase: the way a question is worded
expressions.usage: 수동태 관계절로 질문의 표현 방식을 가리키는 구조

patterns.pattern: Even with [acknowledged solution], [another factor] can dramatically [affect] [the outcome]
patterns.explanation: 앞서 논의된 해결책을 양보 구문으로 인정한 뒤, 또 다른 문제를 제기하며 새로운 비판 포인트로 전환하는 패턴.
examples: Even with perfect ingredients, the way a dish is prepared can dramatically alter the flavor it delivers.
Even with strong evidence, the way a case is presented can dramatically influence the verdict it receives.

drillQuestions.question: Even with careful sampling, the way a question is _____ can shape the answers.
"Even with" signals that careful sampling is _____ but not sufficient.
"Dramatically shape" means to _____ influence.
This sentence introduces a new concern: question _____.

sentences.notes: 논의의 전환점으로, "Even with ~"로 표집 문제를 양보한 뒤 질문 프레이밍이라는 새로운 비판 기준을 도입한다. 비교적 짧은 문장이지만 양보구와 수동태 관계절이 결합된 효율적 구조이다.

koreanAlignment.en: Even with careful sampling
the way a question is worded
can dramatically shape
the answers it produces

pronunciationNotes: dramatically: /drəˈmætɪkli/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "드러매티클리"에 가깝다.
sampling: /ˈsæmplɪŋ/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "샘플링"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Asking "Do you support funding for public parks?" will yield a very different result from asking "Should taxpayers pay more to maintain parks?" — both touch the same topic, but the framing activates different associations in the respondent's mind.
sentences.translation: "공원에 대한 공적 자금 지원을 지지하십니까?"라고 묻는 것은 "납세자가 공원 유지를 위해 더 많은 돈을 내야 합니까?"라고 묻는 것과 매우 다른 결과를 낳을 것이다 — 둘 다 같은 주제를 다루지만, 프레이밍이 응답자의 마음속에서 다른 연상을 활성화시킨다.

grammarElements.text: Asking "Do you support funding for public parks?"
grammarElements.label: 주어 (동명사구 + 직접 인용)

grammarElements.text: a very different result from asking "Should taxpayers pay more to maintain parks?"
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (비교 전치사구 포함)

grammarElements.text: but the framing activates different associations in the respondent's mind
grammarElements.label: but 대조절

vocabulary.example: Different question wordings can yield very different results.
The framing of a question influences how people respond.
The word "taxpayer" activates associations with financial burden.

expressions.phrase: yield a very different result from
expressions.usage: 두 가지 접근의 결과 차이를 비교할 때 쓰는 격식적 표현
인지심리학에서 자극이 관련 개념을 떠올리게 하는 과정을 설명하는 학술적 표현

patterns.pattern: Asking "[Question A]" will yield a very different result from asking "[Question B]" — both touch the same topic, but [the factor] activates different [responses]
patterns.explanation: 두 개의 질문을 직접 인용으로 대비시킨 뒤, 대시 뒤에서 같은 주제임에도 프레이밍이 다른 결과를 만든다는 점을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: Asking "Do you want cleaner air?" will yield a very different result from asking "Should factories be shut down?" — both touch the same topic, but the framing triggers different emotional reactions.

Asking "Should we invest in education?" will yield a very different result from asking "Should taxes increase for schools?" — both touch the same topic, but the wording emphasizes different trade-offs.

drillQuestions.question: Two different questions on the same topic will _____ different results.
The _____ of a question activates different associations.
"Both touch the same topic" means the questions share the same _____.
"Associations in the respondent's mind" refers to mental _____.

sentences.notes: 프레이밍 편향의 구체적 예시를 두 개의 직접 인용 질문으로 보여주는 핵심 문장. "yield a very different result"로 결과의 차이를 강조하고, 대시 뒤에 "both ... but"으로 같은 주제에서 다른 결과가 나오는 역설을 설명한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Asking "Do you support funding for public parks?"
koreanAlignment.ko: "공원에 대한 공적 자금 지원을 지지하십니까?"라고 묻는 것은

koreanAlignment.en: will yield a very different result
from asking "Should taxpayers pay more to maintain parks?"
koreanAlignment.ko: "납세자가 공원 유지를 위해 더 많은 돈을 내야 합니까?"라고 묻는 것과

koreanAlignment.en: both touch the same topic
but the framing activates different associations in the respondent's mind
koreanAlignment.ko: 프레이밍이 응답자의 마음속에서 다른 연상을 활성화시킨다

pronunciationNotes: yield: /jiːld/ — 한 음절. "이일드"에 가깝다. y 다음의 ie는 /iː/로 발음.
associations: /əˌsoʊsiˈeɪʃənz/ — 네 번째 음절에 강세. "어소우시에이션즈"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: Researchers call this "question-order effect" and "framing bias," and reputable surveys go through multiple rounds of testing to minimize these distortions.
sentences.translation: 연구자들은 이를 "질문 순서 효과"와 "프레이밍 편향"이라 부르며, 평판 있는 설문조사는 이러한 왜곡을 최소화하기 위해 여러 차례의 테스트를 거친다.

grammarElements.text: and reputable surveys go through multiple rounds of testing to minimize these distortions
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (to 부정사 목적)

vocabulary.example: Only reputable surveys use rigorous methods.
Question wording can introduce distortions into the data.
Good survey design aims to minimize bias.

expressions.phrase: go through multiple rounds of testing
expressions.usage: 반복적 검증 과정을 나타내는 표현
질문의 배치 순서가 응답에 영향을 미치는 현상을 가리키는 학술 용어

patterns.pattern: Researchers call this "[technical term A]" and "[technical term B]," and [credible sources] go through [process] to minimize these [problems]
patterns.explanation: 학술 용어를 명명하고, 그 문제를 해결하기 위한 전문적 과정을 소개하는 패턴.
examples: Scientists call this "observer bias" and "experimenter effect," and reliable labs follow strict protocols to minimize these influences.
Psychologists call this "anchoring effect" and "availability bias," and careful researchers use control groups to minimize these errors.

drillQuestions.question: Researchers call this the "question-order effect" and "framing _____."
Reputable surveys go through multiple rounds of _____ to minimize distortions.
"Reputable" means having a good _____.
"Distortions" are _____ of the true results.

sentences.notes: 학술 용어(question-order effect, framing bias)를 도입하며 프레이밍 문제에 학문적 근거를 부여한다. "reputable surveys"와 "multiple rounds of testing"이 전문적 해결 과정을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Researchers call this
"question-order effect" and "framing bias"
and reputable surveys go through multiple rounds of testing
koreanAlignment.ko: 평판 있는 설문조사는 여러 차례의 테스트를 거친다

koreanAlignment.en: to minimize these distortions

pronunciationNotes: reputable: /ˈrepjətəbəl/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "레퓨터블"에 가깝다. re-에 강세가 온다.
distortions: /dɪˈstɔːrʃənz/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "디스토션즈"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: The margin of error is another detail that headlines almost always omit but that readers should always look for.
sentences.translation: 오차 범위는 헤드라인이 거의 항상 생략하지만 독자가 항상 찾아봐야 하는 또 다른 세부 사항이다.

grammarElements.text: but that readers should always look for
grammarElements.label: 관계절 2 (but 대조)

vocabulary.example: Every survey should report its margin of error.
Headlines often omit important context.
This detail is critical for understanding the results.

expressions.usage: 언론의 일관된 부주의를 비판적으로 지적하는 표현
독자에게 직접적으로 행동을 권유하는 처방적 표현

patterns.pattern: [Technical term] is another detail that [media] almost always omit but that [readers] should always look for
patterns.explanation: 언론이 생략하는 것과 독자가 찾아야 하는 것을 대조하며, 새로운 비판 기준을 도입하는 패턴. "almost always" vs. "should always"의 대칭이 핵심이다.
examples: The funding source is another detail that articles almost always omit but that readers should always look for.
The sample size is another detail that news reports almost always omit but that informed citizens should always look for.

drillQuestions.question: The margin of error is another detail that headlines almost always _____.
Readers _____ always look for the margin of error.
"Margin of error" measures the _____ of a survey result.
"Another detail" signals that this is _____ the first concern discussed.

sentences.notes: 새로운 비판 기준(오차 범위)을 도입하는 전환 문장. 이중 관계절(that ~ omit but that ~ look for)의 대칭 구조가 언론의 관행과 독자의 의무를 대비시킨다. "almost always"와 "should always"의 빈도부사 반복이 리듬감을 준다.

koreanAlignment.en: The margin of error
is another detail
that headlines almost always omit
but that readers should always look for

pronunciationNotes: margin: /ˈmɑːrdʒɪn/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "마진"에 가깝다.
omit: /oʊˈmɪt/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "오밋"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: A survey reporting that 52% of people favor a policy, with a margin of error of plus or minus four percentage points, is actually saying that the true figure could be anywhere between 48% and 56% — which means the result is essentially a coin flip.
sentences.translation: 정책을 지지하는 사람이 52%라고 보도하는 설문조사가 오차 범위 ±4%포인트라면, 실제로는 진짜 수치가 48%에서 56% 사이 어디든 될 수 있다는 것을 말하는 것이다 — 이는 결과가 본질적으로 동전 던지기와 같다는 뜻이다.

grammarElements.text: A survey reporting that 52% of people favor a policy
grammarElements.label: 주어 (현재분사 수식 명사구 + that절)

grammarElements.text: with a margin of error of plus or minus four percentage points
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (조건부 수식)

grammarElements.text: that the true figure could be anywhere between 48% and 56%
grammarElements.label: that 명사절 (가능성 표현)

grammarElements.text: which means the result is essentially a coin flip
grammarElements.label: 계속적 관계절 (비유적 결론)

vocabulary.example: The margin of error is plus or minus three percentage points.
The true figure may differ from the reported one.
The two results are essentially the same.

expressions.phrase: plus or minus four percentage points
expressions.usage: 통계적 오차 범위를 나타내는 표준 표현
50 대 50에 가까운 불확실한 결과를 일상적 비유로 표현

patterns.pattern: A survey reporting [X%] with a margin of error of [±N], is actually saying that [the range] — which means [vivid analogy]
patterns.explanation: 구체적 수치와 오차 범위를 제시한 후, "is actually saying"으로 재해석하고, 계속적 관계절의 비유로 독자가 직관적으로 이해할 수 있게 만드는 패턴.
examples: A poll showing 51% approval with a margin of error of three points is actually saying that support could range from 48% to 54% — which means the lead is statistically meaningless.
A study reporting a 5% improvement with a margin of error of six points is actually saying the effect could be negative — which means the finding proves nothing.

drillQuestions.question: 52% with a margin of error of ±4 means the true figure could be between 48% and ___%.
"Essentially a coin flip" means the result is close to _____.
"Is actually saying" implies that the headline is _____.
"Percentage points" are different from _____.

sentences.notes: 오차 범위의 실질적 의미를 구체적 수치로 보여주는 핵심 문장. 48%~56%라는 범위를 제시해 52%라는 수치의 불확실성을 드러내고, "a coin flip"이라는 일상적 비유로 통계적 개념을 직관적으로 전달한다. "is actually saying"이 헤드라인과 현실의 간극을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: A survey reporting that 52% of people favor a policy
with a margin of error of plus or minus four percentage points
is actually saying that the true figure could be anywhere between 48% and 56%
koreanAlignment.ko: 실제로는 진짜 수치가 48%에서 56% 사이 어디든 될 수 있다는 것을 말하는 것이다

koreanAlignment.en: which means the result is essentially a coin flip
koreanAlignment.ko: 이는 결과가 본질적으로 동전 던지기와 같다는 뜻이다

pronunciationNotes: essentially: /ɪˈsenʃəli/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "이센셜리"에 가깝다.
percentage: /pərˈsentɪdʒ/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "퍼센티지"에 가깝다.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 수치 재해석을 통한 비유 (numerical reinterpretation via analogy)

sentences.original: Response rates matter as well: if only fifteen percent of the people contacted actually completed the survey, the opinions of the silent eighty-five percent remain unknown, and there is no guarantee that they share the same views.
sentences.translation: 응답률도 중요하다: 연락된 사람들 중 오직 15%만이 실제로 설문조사를 완료했다면, 침묵하는 85%의 의견은 알 수 없으며, 그들이 같은 견해를 공유한다는 보장은 없다.

grammarElements.text: if only fifteen percent of the people contacted actually completed the survey
grammarElements.label: if 조건절 (과거분사 수식 + 부사 강조)

grammarElements.text: the opinions of the silent eighty-five percent remain unknown, and there is no guarantee that they share the same views
grammarElements.label: 주절 (병렬 + that 명사절)

vocabulary.example: Low response rates undermine the reliability of survey results.
There is no guarantee that non-respondents share the same views.
The silent majority may hold very different views.

expressions.usage: 추가적인 중요 요소를 도입할 때 쓰는 간결한 표현
확실성의 부재를 강조하는 격식적 표현

patterns.pattern: [Factor] matter as well: if only [low percentage] of [contacted group] actually [responded], the [silent percentage] remain unknown, and there is no guarantee that [they agree]
patterns.explanation: 콜론 뒤에 조건절로 구체적 수치를 제시하고, 미응답자의 의견이 불확실하다는 논점을 "no guarantee"로 마무리하는 패턴.
examples: Participation rates matter as well: if only twenty percent of invited employees actually attended, the preferences of the absent eighty percent remain unknown, and there is no guarantee that they would have voted the same way.

Completion rates matter as well: if only ten percent of enrolled students actually finished, the experience of the ninety percent who dropped out remains unknown, and there is no guarantee that they faced the same challenges.

drillQuestions.question: If only fifteen percent completed the survey, the opinions of the _____ eighty-five percent remain unknown.
There is no _____ that non-respondents share the same views.
"Response rates matter as well" introduces a _____ concern.
"Actually completed" emphasizes that many people _____ complete surveys.

sentences.notes: 응답률이라는 새로운 비판 기준을 도입하는 문장. 콜론 뒤에 "if only fifteen percent"라는 구체적 수치로 문제의 심각성을 부각하고, "the silent eighty-five percent"라는 표현이 미응답자 편향의 위험을 생생하게 전달한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Response rates matter as well
if only fifteen percent of the people contacted actually completed the survey
koreanAlignment.ko: 연락된 사람들 중 오직 15%만이 실제로 설문조사를 완료했다면

koreanAlignment.en: the opinions of the silent eighty-five percent remain unknown
and there is no guarantee that they share the same views
koreanAlignment.ko: 그들이 같은 견해를 공유한다는 보장은 없다

pronunciationNotes: guarantee: /ˌɡærənˈtiː/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "개런티"에 가깝다.
response: /rɪˈspɑːns/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "리스판스"에 가깝다.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 수치를 통한 문제 부각 (numerical problem highlighting)

sentences.original: Finally, consider who funded the survey and what they might have hoped to find, because organizations sometimes design studies to produce results that support a predetermined conclusion.
sentences.translation: 마지막으로, 누가 설문조사에 자금을 대었고 무엇을 발견하기를 바랐을 수 있는지를 고려하라, 왜냐하면 조직들은 때때로 미리 정해진 결론을 뒷받침하는 결과를 만들어 내기 위해 연구를 설계하기 때문이다.

grammarElements.text: and what they might have hoped to find
grammarElements.label: 목적어 2 (간접의문문 + 완료 부정사)

grammarElements.text: because organizations sometimes design studies to produce results that support a predetermined conclusion
grammarElements.label: because 이유절 (to 부정사 + 관계절)

vocabulary.example: Who funded the study is a critical question.
The conclusion was predetermined before the study began.
Some studies are designed to prove a specific point.

expressions.phrase: what they might have hoped to find
expressions.usage: "might have hoped"으로 출자자의 숨겨진 의도를 신중하게 추론하는 표현
연구가 시작되기 전에 이미 결론이 있었음을 시사하는 비판적 표현

patterns.pattern: Finally, consider [wh-question A] and [wh-question B], because [actors] sometimes [manipulate process] to [achieve biased outcome]
patterns.explanation: 체계적 분석의 마지막 기준을 "Finally"로 도입하고, 명령형(consider)으로 독자에게 직접 행동을 권유하며, because절로 출자자 편향의 메커니즘을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: Finally, consider who published the report and what their agenda might be, because interest groups sometimes commission research to justify positions they already hold.
Finally, consider who benefits from the findings and what they stand to gain, because corporations sometimes sponsor studies to generate favorable publicity.

drillQuestions.question: We should consider who _____ the survey.
Organizations sometimes design studies to support a _____ conclusion.
"Finally" signals this is the _____ concern in the list.
"What they might have hoped to find" suggests the funder had a desired _____.

sentences.notes: 비판적 분석의 마지막 기준(출자자 편향)을 도입하는 문장. 명령형(consider)이 독자를 직접 참여시키고, "might have hoped"이라는 완화 표현이 단정을 피하면서도 의심의 씨앗을 심는다. "predetermined conclusion"이 편향의 의도성을 날카롭게 지적한다.

koreanAlignment.en: Finally, consider
who funded the survey
and what they might have hoped to find
because organizations sometimes design studies
koreanAlignment.ko: 왜냐하면 조직들은 때때로 연구를 설계하기 때문이다

koreanAlignment.en: to produce results that support a predetermined conclusion
koreanAlignment.ko: 미리 정해진 결론을 뒷받침하는 결과를 만들어 내기 위해

pronunciationNotes: predetermined: /ˌpriːdɪˈtɜːrmɪnd/ — 세 번째 음절에 강세. "프리디터민드"에 가깝다. pre- 접두사가 "미리"를 뜻한다.
funded: /ˈfʌndɪd/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "펀디드"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: None of this means that surveys are useless — when conducted rigorously, they remain one of the best tools for understanding public opinion at scale.
sentences.translation: 이 중 어느 것도 설문조사가 무용하다는 뜻은 아니다 — 엄격하게 실시될 때, 설문조사는 대규모로 여론을 이해하기 위한 최고의 도구 중 하나로 남는다.

grammarElements.text: they remain one of the best tools for understanding public opinion at scale
grammarElements.label: 주절 (대시 뒤 긍정적 재평가)

vocabulary.example: The author emphasizes that surveys are not useless.
Research must be conducted rigorously to be trustworthy.
Surveys allow us to understand public opinion at scale.

expressions.meaning: 이 중 어느 것도 ~을 의미하지는 않는다
expressions.usage: 앞선 비판이 전면 부정이 아님을 명시하여 균형을 잡는 표현

expressions.phrase: one of the best tools for
expressions.usage: 가치를 인정하면서도 유일한 도구는 아님을 암시하는 표현

patterns.pattern: None of this means that [subject] is [entirely negative] — when [done well], [they] remain one of the best [tools] for [purpose]
patterns.explanation: 앞선 비판을 모두 인정하면서도 전면 부정은 아님을 "None of this means"로 명시하고, 대시 뒤에서 조건(when conducted rigorously)을 달아 긍정적 재평가를 제시하는 균형 잡힌 패턴.
examples: None of this means that social media is harmful — when used mindfully, it remains one of the best tools for connecting people across distances.
None of this means that standardized tests are worthless — when designed carefully, they remain one of the best tools for comparing student achievement across regions.

drillQuestions.question: "None of this means that surveys are _____" shows the author is being balanced.
When conducted _____, surveys remain valuable.
"At scale" means on a _____ level.
"None of this means" prevents the reader from _____ all surveys.

sentences.notes: 논증의 균형을 잡는 핵심 전환 문장. "None of this means"로 앞선 비판이 설문조사 자체의 부정이 아님을 명확히 하고, 대시 뒤에서 "when conducted rigorously"라는 조건을 붙여 긍정적 가치를 재확인한다.

koreanAlignment.en: None of this means that surveys are useless
koreanAlignment.ko: 이 중 어느 것도 설문조사가 무용하다는 뜻은 아니다

koreanAlignment.en: when conducted rigorously
they remain one of the best tools
for understanding public opinion at scale

pronunciationNotes: rigorously: /ˈrɪɡərəsli/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "리거러슬리"에 가깝다.
useless: /ˈjuːsləs/ — 첫 음절에 강세. "유슬리스"에 가깝다. use와 같은 발음.

sentences.original: The point is not to dismiss every number but to develop the habit of asking a few simple questions before accepting it as fact.
sentences.translation: 요점은 모든 숫자를 무시하는 것이 아니라 그것을 사실로 받아들이기 전에 몇 가지 간단한 질문을 하는 습관을 기르는 것이다.

grammarElements.text: but to develop the habit of asking a few simple questions
grammarElements.label: 보어 2 (긍정 to 부정사)

vocabulary.example: The point is not to dismiss all data.
We should develop the habit of reading critically.
Accepting data without question is risky.

expressions.phrase: not to dismiss ... but to develop
expressions.usage: "not A but B" 구조로 에세이의 핵심 메시지를 명확히 하는 표현
비판 없이 정보를 수용하는 행위를 가리키는 표현

patterns.pattern: The point is not to [extreme action] but to [moderate action] before [accepting something uncritically]
patterns.explanation: "not A but B" 구조로 극단적 반응(모든 것을 무시)을 배제하고 적절한 반응(비판적 질문)을 권유하며, 에세이의 핵심 주장을 정리하는 패턴.
examples: The point is not to reject every claim but to verify the evidence before sharing it with others.
The point is not to distrust every politician but to evaluate their record before casting a vote.

drillQuestions.question: The point is not to _____ every number.
Instead, we should develop the _____ of asking questions.
"Before accepting it as fact" means before _____ the number is true.
"Not to A but to B" clarifies the essay's _____ message.

sentences.notes: 에세이의 핵심 주장을 "not to A but to B" 구조로 명확히 정리하는 문장. "dismiss every number"(극단)와 "develop the habit of asking"(적정)을 대비시켜 비판적 읽기가 부정이 아닌 건전한 회의라는 점을 강조한다.

koreanAlignment.en: The point is
not to dismiss every number
but to develop the habit of asking a few simple questions
koreanAlignment.ko: 몇 가지 간단한 질문을 하는 습관을 기르는 것이다

koreanAlignment.en: before accepting it as fact

pronunciationNotes: dismiss: /dɪsˈmɪs/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "디스미스"에 가깝다.
develop: /dɪˈveləp/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "디벨럽"에 가깝다.

sentences.original: A well-informed reader treats survey data the way a careful driver treats a yellow traffic light — not as a signal to stop, but as a reminder to slow down and look more carefully before proceeding.
sentences.translation: 잘 알고 있는 독자는 설문조사 데이터를 신중한 운전자가 노란 신호등을 대하듯 대한다 — 멈추라는 신호가 아니라, 진행하기 전에 속도를 줄이고 더 주의 깊게 살피라는 알림으로.

grammarElements.text: the way a careful driver treats a yellow traffic light
grammarElements.label: 부사구 (비유적 비교)

grammarElements.text: not as a signal to stop, but as a reminder to slow down and look more carefully before proceeding
grammarElements.label: 대시 뒤 "not A but B" 설명구

vocabulary.example: A well-informed citizen asks questions before believing headlines.
She treats every claim with healthy skepticism.
Look carefully before proceeding through the intersection.

expressions.phrase: the way a careful driver treats a yellow traffic light
expressions.usage: 일상적인 비유로 추상적 원칙을 직관적으로 전달하는 비교 표현

expressions.phrase: not as a signal to stop, but as a reminder to slow down
expressions.meaning: 멈추라는 신호가 아니라 속도를 줄이라는 알림으로
expressions.usage: "not A but B"로 적절한 반응의 정도를 명시하는 표현

patterns.pattern: A [well-qualified person] treats [data] the way [careful analogy figure] treats [analogy object] — not as [extreme response], but as a reminder to [moderate response] before [action]
patterns.explanation: 비유적 비교로 글 전체의 메시지를 요약하는 결론 패턴. 대시 뒤의 "not A but B"가 적절한 반응의 수준을 명시한다.
examples: A good scientist treats preliminary findings the way a cautious investor treats market rumors — not as a reason to act immediately, but as a prompt to investigate further before committing.
A thoughtful consumer treats advertising claims the way a seasoned traveler treats tourist guidebooks — not as gospel truth, but as a starting point to explore more carefully before deciding.

drillQuestions.question: A well-informed reader treats survey data like a careful driver treats a _____ traffic light.
The yellow light is not a signal to _____.
It is a reminder to slow down and look more _____ before proceeding.
"Well-informed" means having good _____ about a subject.

sentences.notes: 에세이 전체를 비유로 요약하는 결론 문장. 노란 신호등이라는 일상적 이미지가 비판적 읽기의 본질 — 멈추지도 않고 돌진하지도 않는 — 을 직관적으로 전달한다. "not as A, but as B"가 이 에세이의 핵심 태도(건전한 회의)를 최종적으로 정리한다.

koreanAlignment.en: A well-informed reader treats survey data
koreanAlignment.ko: 잘 알고 있는 독자는 설문조사 데이터를 대한다

koreanAlignment.en: the way a careful driver treats a yellow traffic light
not as a signal to stop
but as a reminder to slow down and look more carefully
koreanAlignment.ko: 속도를 줄이고 더 주의 깊게 살피라는 알림으로

koreanAlignment.en: before proceeding

pronunciationNotes: well-informed: /ˌwelɪnˈfɔːrmd/ — informed의 두 번째 음절에 강세. "웰인폼드"에 가깝다.
proceeding: /prəˈsiːdɪŋ/ — 두 번째 음절에 강세. "프러시딩"에 가깝다. proceed와 precede의 발음 차이에 주의.
